name: known-hosts
context: Verify SSH server identity with known_hosts, fingerprints, key rotation, and safe first contact
info: This node covers the server-trust side of SSH: first-use host verification, `known_hosts` lookup and maintenance, hashed hostnames, changed-host-key response, `StrictHostKeyChecking`, `UpdateHostKeys`, and optional `VerifyHostKeyDNS`. Agents need this whenever they connect to new infrastructure, rotate host keys, or troubleshoot the classic 'REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED' failure without blindly disabling verification.
triggers: accept a new ssh host key safely
fix remote host identification has changed
body:
# known_hosts and Host Verification

SSH remembers server host keys in `~/.ssh/known_hosts` and optionally `/etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts`. On first contact, the client asks whether to trust the server key unless stricter policy is configured. On later connections, the key must still match or the connection is treated as suspicious.

## First Contact

The default behavior is usually `StrictHostKeyChecking ask`, which prompts before adding a new key. For unattended automation where first contact is acceptable, many agents use `accept-new` instead of disabling checks entirely:

```bash
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ops@host.example.com
```

## Inspect and Remove Entries

```bash
ssh-keygen -F host.example.com
ssh-keygen -R host.example.com
ssh-keygen -H -f ~/.ssh/known_hosts
```

- `-F` finds entries, including hashed names.
- `-R` removes entries for a host, which is the safe repair after legitimate host-key rotation.
- `-H` hashes hostnames in place and writes a `.old` backup.

## Rotation and Alternate Verification

If you already trust a server key, `UpdateHostKeys` can learn replacement keys and support graceful rotation. DNS-based SSHFP records can also help when used carefully:

```bash
ssh-keygen -r host.example.com
ssh -o VerifyHostKeyDNS=ask host.example.com
```

Do not 'fix' host-key errors by turning verification off globally. Load [[ssh/troubleshooting]] instead and confirm whether the change is expected.

sections.title: First contact and StrictHostKeyChecking
sections.body: `StrictHostKeyChecking yes` never adds unknown hosts automatically. `accept-new` adds new keys but still blocks changed ones. `ask` is the common default and prompts before adding new hosts. `no` or `off` is the loosest setting and should be a last resort. Use [[ssh/config]] to scope any policy to specific hosts instead of weakening every connection.

sections.title: Changed host keys
sections.body: A changed host key may indicate legitimate rebuild or rotation, or it may indicate MITM risk. Confirm the new fingerprint out-of-band, then remove the old entry with `ssh-keygen -R host` and reconnect. SSH intentionally disables easy fallback here because host-key continuity is the integrity check.

sections.title: Hashing hostnames and learning new keys
sections.body: Use `ssh-keygen -H -f ~/.ssh/known_hosts` to hash hostnames at rest. `UpdateHostKeys yes|ask` lets trusted servers advertise additional host keys so clients can learn replacements before old keys disappear. `UserKnownHostsFile` controls which file the client updates. See [[ssh/config]].

links.context: Host-key policy affects every SSH connection
Set StrictHostKeyChecking, UpdateHostKeys, and UserKnownHostsFile in config
Distinguish host keys from client identity keys

related.note: Trusted host keys are required before a safe SSH session can proceed
Client config controls host-key verification policy
Client identity keys and server host keys solve different trust problems

author: dojo-community
